recommended_model.name: thread-primitive
recommended_model.summary: Promote `thread_id` to a first-class, channel-scoped envelope field that groups conversation. Keep `interaction_id` but narrow its semantics to a directed bilateral work-lifecycle that always lives inside a thread. Channels stay as routing/visibility namespaces; reply_to/trace_id/causation_id stay unchanged. Direct peer rooms collapse into derived filtered views over (channel, thread) state, not a parallel storage model.
why_this_wins: Threads and interactions answer different questions: threads are conversation grouping (many participants, no lifecycle, the Slack-thread shape), interactions are directed bilateral work (two peers, state machine, the RPC shape). Conflating them either drops the lifecycle (weakening directed handoffs) or forces broadcast chat into a 2-party lifecycle (weakening the channel as a town-square). Two sharp primitives beat one overloaded one.

Channel-scoped threads are routing-cheap: NATS subjects do not change, the wire stays `agh.network.v0.<channel>.broadcast | peer.<route_token>`, and `thread_id` is just envelope metadata. The protocol remains implementable outside AGH (preserves the moat statement in the glossary).

Multiple interactions can co-exist in one thread (e.g., one thread spawns three concurrent directed handoffs). Multiple threads can co-exist in one channel without collision. This is what operators actually need when humans + multiple agents share one room.

Greenfield-hard-cut friendly: zero compatibility shims, one schema migration, one envelope field, one new store table. AGORA-spec drafts already had `thread`; this restores a load-bearing field that was dropped without justification.

Agent-manageability is preserved end-to-end. CLI verbs gain `--thread`, HTTP/UDS gain thread routes, the bundled skill tells agents how to find and answer in the active thread. No web-only manageability, no UI-only threading.

The cost of overloading `interaction_id` (Option 2) is asymmetric: chat traffic without lifecycle (`say`) would be forced into 2-peer constraints, terminal-state rules, and `not_target` rejection paths that have nothing to do with conversation grouping. The cost of a new field (Option 1) is one column, one validator, and one envelope key.

alternatives_considered.name: interaction-becomes-thread
pros: No new envelope field; minimal wire change.
Vocabulary stays familiar to anyone who already learned the v0 RFC.
cons: Either drops the directed bilateral lifecycle (loses handoff/RPC semantics that already work) or imposes that lifecycle on multi-participant broadcast chat (wrong shape for `say`).
Two-peer participation rule (only initiator + target) becomes a thread participation rule, which is exactly what threads must NOT have.
Terminal states (`completed | failed | canceled`) on a chat thread are nonsensical; you'd need new rules just to disable them for `say`.
Loses the ability to nest multiple directed handoffs inside one conversational topic — every handoff would silently terminate the conversation.
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: It pretends conversation grouping and bilateral RPC are the same primitive. They are not. Forcing them together produces a chimera that breaks at the first multi-agent thread with overlapping handoffs.

alternatives_considered.name: ui-only-threading-on-reply_to-chains
pros: Already partially possible by following `reply_to` graphs.
cons: `reply_to` graphs cannot reliably reconstruct a thread under fanout, out-of-order delivery, or reply skips. Slack tried this once and abandoned it.
Two clients reconstructing the same `reply_to` graph can disagree on which messages belong to a thread.
Agents have no canonical handle to address a thread they are participating in.
Server-side query for 'all messages in thread T' becomes a recursive walk instead of a single index lookup.
Fails the 'agent-manageable' bar: you can't `agh network send --thread T` because T does not exist as a first-class identity.
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Replicates a known industry failure mode. Threads must have a stable, server-known identity, not an inferred one.

alternatives_considered.name: thread-as-its-own-channel
pros: No new envelope field; threads are just sub-channels.
Existing channel-scoping logic and NATS subjects handle isolation for free.
cons: Channel grammar is `[a-z0-9][a-z0-9_-]{0,63}` and is human-named. Threads are typically auto-generated identifiers and would either pollute the channel namespace or require a totally new naming rule.
Cross-thread search inside one logical channel becomes cross-subject, which is operationally hostile.
Loses the parent/child relationship between channel and thread — threads belong somewhere; sub-channels don't.
Membership/discovery (`greet`, `whois`) gets duplicated per thread; presence noise multiplies.
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Confuses physical transport segmentation with logical conversation grouping. Channels are the right level for routing/visibility; threads are the right level for conversation segmentation.

alternatives_considered.name: topic-tags-instead-of-threads
pros: Operators can attach multiple topic tags to a message.
cons: No canonical answer to 'which thread is this message part of' — a message in N tags belongs to N threads, breaking unread state, RPC semantics, and reply lanes.
Cannot model Slack-thread ergonomics where a message is in exactly one thread.
Forces every consumer to implement set-based threading from scratch.
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Many-to-many is the wrong cardinality for the operator and agent mental model the product needs.

core_invariants: A thread is a conversation grouping inside exactly one channel. Threads do not span channels.
Thread identity is `(channel, thread_id)`. The same `thread_id` string in different channels is a different thread.
A thread has no lifecycle. Threads are durable conversation containers; they do not 'close', 'fail', or 'cancel'.
An interaction is a directed bilateral work unit and MUST live inside a thread: `interaction_id` requires `thread_id`. Interactions retain their existing state machine and 2-peer participation rule.
A thread MAY contain zero, one, or many concurrent interactions. Interactions do not own the thread; they are scoped sub-containers.
Any peer present in the channel MAY post any conversational kind into any thread in that channel. Threads do not gate participation; channels do.

Threads are explicitly opened. The first conversational envelope in a channel that carries a fresh `thread_id` opens that thread. There is no implicit reply-to-thread coercion in v0; later RFCs may define it.
`reply_to` semantics are unchanged — a per-message reply pointer that may point inside or outside the same thread.
`trace_id` and `causation_id` semantics are unchanged — distributed correlation, not conversation grouping.
Direct peer rooms are derived views over thread state, not a parallel primitive. The protocol has channels and threads; 'DM with peer X' is a UI filter, not a wire concept.
Greet and whois are presence/discovery and never carry `thread_id`.

envelope_changes: Add `thread_id: string | null` to the v0 envelope alongside `interaction_id`. Length and shape match `interaction_id`: collision-resistant, peer-generated, opaque to the protocol.

Per-kind requirements: `say` MAY carry `thread_id` (null = channel root timeline); `direct` MUST carry `thread_id` AND `interaction_id`; `capability` MUST carry `thread_id` (interaction_id remains conditional per current rules); `receipt` MUST carry `thread_id` AND inherit `interaction_id` from the message it acknowledges; `trace` MUST carry `thread_id` AND `interaction_id`; `greet` MUST NOT carry `thread_id`; `whois` SHOULD NOT carry `thread_id` (whois is peer-scoped, not thread-scoped).
Validation rule: if `interaction_id` is present, `thread_id` MUST be present. Reject as `malformed` otherwise.
Reuse the existing `[a-z0-9][a-z0-9._-]{0,127}` peer_id grammar for `thread_id` (deterministic, subject-safe, no surprises).
No new top-level fields besides `thread_id`. `reply_to`, `trace_id`, `causation_id`, `interaction_id` are unchanged on the wire.
lifecycle_changes: Thread has no protocol lifecycle. Storage MAY track `last_activity_at`, `participant_count`, `message_count` for indexing, but those are not protocol states.
Interaction lifecycle (`submitted | working | needs_input | completed | failed | canceled`) survives unchanged and applies only when `interaction_id` is present.

Multiple interactions inside one thread are independent: each has its own state machine, its own initiator/target pair, and its own terminal-state rules. Closing one interaction does not close the thread.

Add registry rule: a `direct`/`capability` arriving with a `thread_id` for an unknown thread implicitly opens the thread (lightweight side effect, no extra round trip). The first envelope's `from`/`to` does not bind thread membership.
routing_changes: NATS subject mapping is UNCHANGED. Threads are envelope-level metadata; transport stays `agh.network.v0.<channel>.broadcast` and `agh.network.v0.<channel>.peer.<route_token>`.
Receivers index inbound envelopes by `(channel, thread_id)` in addition to `(channel, peer)` and `(channel, interaction_id)`.
The router's interaction-membership check (initiator+target only) is preserved verbatim for `interaction_id`-bearing envelopes. Threads do not add a parallel membership check.
Router replay and dedup keys (`id`-based) are unchanged.
querying_changes: Add `(channel, thread_id)` as a primary index in the persisted timeline. Listing a thread is a single scan.
Channel timeline query gains an `include_threads` flag with three values: `none` (root only), `roots` (root messages plus first-line preview of each thread), `all` (flattened).
Add `since_thread_message_id` cursor to support thread-scoped pagination.
Capability discovery (`whois` + `agh.capability_catalog`) does not interact with threads; it remains peer-scoped.
visibility_rules: All threads in a channel are visible to all peers in that channel. The protocol does not define private threads in v0; if needed later, that is a new RFC concern.
Trust state (`verified | unverified | rejected`) is per-envelope, not per-thread. A thread can contain a mix of trust-classed messages.
Untrusted-input wrapping (the existing prompt-injection defense) carries through into thread-scoped delivery; the daemon's prompt formatter must include `thread_id` in the wrapper.
Thread-level moderation, banning, or muting is out of scope for v0.

router_and_store: `internal/network/router.go` indexes envelopes by `(channel, thread_id)` in addition to existing keys; the existing `interactions map[string]Interaction` keyed by `(channel, interaction_id)` stays.

Add `internal/network/threads.go` with a `ThreadRegistry` that stores opened-at, opener, last-activity, message-count, and participating peers (set, not lifecycle). In-memory and reconstructable, mirroring the existing peer registry pattern.

Add a numbered SQLite migration (per `agh-schema-migration`) that: (1) adds `thread_id TEXT NULL` column to `network_timeline_log`, (2) creates `network_threads (channel TEXT, thread_id TEXT, opened_at, last_activity_at, opened_by, root_message_id, message_count, participant_count, PRIMARY KEY (channel, thread_id))`, (3) creates an index `(channel, thread_id, timestamp)` on `network_timeline_log`. Greenfield-hard-cut: no backfill of NULL `thread_id`s; existing rows stay NULL and are read as 'channel root'.
Audit log (`network_audit_log`) gains `thread_id` so post-incident reconstruction can scope to a thread.
delivery_and_prompting: Per-session inbound queue is split per `(thread_id)` within each channel. Drainage order: oldest message wins across threads (no per-thread starvation), but the prompt wrapper carries the active thread tag so the agent knows which thread it is being asked about.

Prompt wrapper format extends the existing `<network-message>` element: add `thread="<thread_id>"` attribute. If multiple threads have queued messages, the daemon may inject a `<network-thread-summary>` envelope listing thread heads before delivering the next batch (out-of-band hint, not protocol).
Sending without `thread_id` in a `direct` is a hard error returned to the agent (deterministic CLI/UDS error: `thread_required`).

Auto-thread on reply: if an agent uses a CLI `--reply-to <message_id>` flag without `--thread`, the daemon resolves the parent message's `thread_id` and uses it. This is a runtime ergonomic, not a protocol rule.
Prompt back-pressure: the existing 'busy session queues' contract carries through; threads do not change ordering.
orchestration_rules: Coordinator agents (autonomy kernel) bind to `coordination_channel_id` AND a `coordination_thread_id`. One thread per coordinated run, opened on enqueue. This makes the existing 'bind always, speak when useful' contract sharper: speak in the run's thread, not the channel root.
Task ingress guards now match `(network_channel, network_thread)` when the task carries a thread binding; mismatch is a `wrong_channel_or_thread` rejection.
Capability handoff (`kind:capability`) opens a thread by default if the sender did not pre-open one — this is what most operators expect when they 'hand off a piece of work'.
Hooks gain `network.thread.opened`, `network.thread.message` typed events. Existing call-site dispatch rules apply (no event tailing).
observability: Every canonical event with network correlation gains a `network_thread_id` field alongside the existing `network_channel`.
SSE stream payloads add `thread_id` so the web can re-render the thread rail without polling.

`/api/diagnostics/status` network metrics gain per-thread counts (active threads, threads-with-pending-replies). Bounded by `network.threads.max_indexed_per_channel` config to prevent DoS by thread-spam.
Replay/transcript assembly groups by thread first, then by causation. The existing `transcript` package gets a thread-aware mode.

cli: `agh network send` adds `--thread <id>` (required for `direct`/`capability`/`trace`/`receipt`, optional for `say`).
`agh network send` adds `--open-thread` (generates a fresh `thread_id`, returns it to stdout, also used as the message's `thread_id`). Used by orchestrators starting work.
`agh network send` resolves `--reply-to` to its parent's `thread_id` when `--thread` is not provided; documents this in `--help`.
New verb: `agh network threads <channel> [--peer <id>] [--since <ts>] [-o json|jsonl]` — list threads in a channel.
New verb: `agh network thread <channel> <thread_id> [--limit] [--cursor] [-o json|jsonl]` — show messages in one thread.
`agh network inbox` output includes a `thread_id` column (text, jsonl). Adds `--thread <id>` filter.
Bash/zsh/fish completions gain dynamic thread completion via UDS introspection.
http_uds_api: Add `GET /api/network/channels/{channel}/threads/{thread_id}` returning thread detail (opened_at, opener, participants, root message preview, counts).
Modify `POST /api/network/send` to require `thread_id` per the per-kind rules; return `thread_id` on success.
